concepts.name: symmetric multiclass logistic model
concepts.description: A version of multiclass logistic regression where all K classes have their own coefficient vectors, with regularization forcing Σˆβ_lj = 0.
concepts.existing_quote: With K classes, we use a symmetric version of the multiclass logistic model (4.17) on page 119: Pr(G = k|X = x) = exp(βk0 + x^Tβk)/Σ exp(βl0 + x^Tβl)

paragraphs.text:
This is page 649
Printer: Opaque this
18
High-Dimensional Problems: p ≫N

In this chapter we discuss prediction problems in which the number of
features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written
p ≫N. Such problems have become of increasing importance, especially in
genomics and other areas of computational biology. We will see that high
variance and overﬁtting are a major concern in this setting. As a result,
simple, highly regularized approaches often become the methods of choice.
The ﬁrst part of the chapter focuses on prediction in both the classiﬁcation
and regression settings, while the second part discusses the more basic
problem of feature selection and assessment.
To get us started, Figure 18.1 summarizes a small simulation study that
demonstrates the “less ﬁtting is better” principle that applies when p ≫N.
For each of N = 100 samples, we generated p standard Gaussian features
X with pairwise correlation 0.2. The outcome Y was generated according
to a linear model

where ε was generated from a standard Gaussian distribution. For each
dataset, the set of coeﬃcients βj were also generated from a standard Gaus-
sian distribution. We investigated three cases: p = 20, 100, and 1000. The
standard deviation σ was chosen in each case so that the signal-to-noise
ratio Var[E(Y |X)])/Var(ε) equaled 2. As a result, the number of signiﬁcant

univariate regression coeﬃcients1 was 9, 33 and 331, respectively, averaged
over the 100 simulation runs. The p = 1000 case is designed to mimic the
kind of data that we might see in a high-dimensional genomic or proteomic
dataset, for example.
We ﬁt a ridge regression to the data, with three diﬀerent values for the
regularization parameter λ: 0.001, 100, and 1000. When λ = 0.001, this
is nearly the same as least squares regression, with a little regularization
just to ensure that the problem is non-singular when p > N. Figure 18.1
shows boxplots of the relative test error achieved by the diﬀerent estimators
in each scenario. The corresponding average degrees of freedom used in
each ridge-regression ﬁt is indicated (computed using formula (3.50) on
page 682). The degrees of freedom is a more interpretable parameter than
λ. We see that ridge regression with λ = 0.001 (20 df) wins when p = 20;
λ = 100 (35 df) wins when p = 100, and λ = 1000 (43 df) wins when
p = 1000,
Here is an explanation for these results. When p = 20, we ﬁt all the way
and we can identify as many of the signiﬁcant coeﬃcients as possible with

1We call a regression coeﬃcient signiﬁcant if |bβj/ bsej| ≥2, where ˆβj is the estimated
(univariate) coeﬃcient and bsej is its estimated standard error.
2For a ﬁxed value of the regularization parameter λ, the degrees of freedom depends
on the observed predictor values in each simulation. Hence we compute the average
degrees of freedom over simulations.

concepts.name: one versus one approach
concepts.description: A method for generalizing two-class classifiers to K > 2 classes by computing all pairwise classifiers and using voting to determine the predicted class.
concepts.existing_quote: In the "one versus one" (ovo) approach, we compute all pairwise classifiers. For each test point, the predicted class is the one that wins the most pairwise contests.

concepts.name: one versus all approach
concepts.description: A method for generalizing two-class classifiers to K > 2 classes by comparing each class to all others in K two-class comparisons, choosing the class with highest confidence.
concepts.existing_quote: In the "one versus all" (ova) approach, each class is compared to all of the others in K two-class comparisons. To classify a test point, we compute the confidences (signed distance from the hyperplane) for each of the K classifiers. The winner is the class with the highest confidence.

concepts.name: fused lasso
concepts.description: A modification of the lasso for functional data that penalizes both the L1 norm of coefficients and the L1 norm of their successive differences, encouraging sparsity and smoothness.
concepts.existing_quote: The fused lasso (Tibshirani et al., 2005) solves min_β Σ(yi - Σβjxij)^2 + λ1Σ|βj| + λ2Σ|βj - βj-1|. The first penalty encourages the solution to be sparse, while the second encourages it to be smooth in the index j.

concepts.name: string kernel
concepts.description: A similarity measure for variable-length strings based on counting matching substrings, computing inner products K(x1,x2) = Σφ_a(x1)φ_a(x2) where φ_a counts occurrences of substring a.
concepts.existing_quote: Using this, we define the inner product K(x1,x2) = Σ_{a∈A^m} φ_a(x1)φ_a(x2) which measures the similarity between the two strings x1, x2.

concepts.name: bag of words representation
concepts.description: A document representation where features x_ij are counts of how many times word j appears in document i, discarding word order.
concepts.existing_quote: We extracted all unique words from these abstracts, and defined features xij to be the number of times word j appears in abstract i. This is the so-called bag of words representation.

concepts.name: double-centered kernel
concepts.description: Computing principal components from an uncentered inner-product matrix by centering: (I - M)K(I - M) where M is the mean operator.
concepts.existing_quote: Thus we compute the eigenvectors of the double-centered kernel (I −M)K(I −M) for the principal components from an uncentered inner-product matrix.

concepts.name: supervised principal components
concepts.description: A method for high-dimensional regression that filters features by univariate significance, then computes principal components on the reduced set, and uses them in a regression model.
concepts.existing_quote: Supervised principal components is a simple approach to regression and generalized regression that is especially useful when p ≫N. We restrict attention to features which by themselves have a sizable correlation with the outcome.

concepts.name: kaplan-meier method
concepts.description: A method for estimating survival curves from censored survival data.
concepts.existing_quote: Figure 18.12 shows the survival curve estimated by the Kaplan–Meier method for the 80 patients in the test set.

concepts.name: latent variable model for supervised pca
concepts.description: A single-component factor analysis model where outcome Y relates to latent factor U, and relevant features X_j also relate to U through linear models.
concepts.existing_quote: Suppose we have a response variable Y which is related to an underlying latent variable U by a linear model Y = β20 + β21U + ε. In addition, we have measurements on a set of features Xj indexed by j ∈P (for pathway), for which Xj = β10j + β11jU + ϵj.

paragraphs.text:
This is page 649
Printer: Opaque this
18
High-Dimensional Problems: p ≫N

In this chapter we discuss prediction problems in which the number of
features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written
p ≫N. Such problems have become of increasing importance, especially in
genomics and other areas of computational biology. We will see that high
variance and overﬁtting are a major concern in this setting. As a result,
simple, highly regularized approaches often become the methods of choice.
The ﬁrst part of the chapter focuses on prediction in both the classiﬁcation
and regression settings, while the second part discusses the more basic
problem of feature selection and assessment.
To get us started, Figure 18.1 summarizes a small simulation study that
demonstrates the “less ﬁtting is better” principle that applies when p ≫N.
For each of N = 100 samples, we generated p standard Gaussian features
X with pairwise correlation 0.2. The outcome Y was generated according
to a linear model

where ε was generated from a standard Gaussian distribution. For each
dataset, the set of coeﬃcients βj were also generated from a standard Gaus-
sian distribution. We investigated three cases: p = 20, 100, and 1000. The
standard deviation σ was chosen in each case so that the signal-to-noise
ratio Var[E(Y |X)])/Var(ε) equaled 2. As a result, the number of signiﬁcant

univariate regression coeﬃcients1 was 9, 33 and 331, respectively, averaged
over the 100 simulation runs. The p = 1000 case is designed to mimic the
kind of data that we might see in a high-dimensional genomic or proteomic
dataset, for example.
We ﬁt a ridge regression to the data, with three diﬀerent values for the
regularization parameter λ: 0.001, 100, and 1000. When λ = 0.001, this
is nearly the same as least squares regression, with a little regularization
just to ensure that the problem is non-singular when p > N. Figure 18.1
shows boxplots of the relative test error achieved by the diﬀerent estimators
in each scenario. The corresponding average degrees of freedom used in
each ridge-regression ﬁt is indicated (computed using formula (3.50) on
page 682). The degrees of freedom is a more interpretable parameter than
λ. We see that ridge regression with λ = 0.001 (20 df) wins when p = 20;
λ = 100 (35 df) wins when p = 100, and λ = 1000 (43 df) wins when
p = 1000,
Here is an explanation for these results. When p = 20, we ﬁt all the way
and we can identify as many of the signiﬁcant coeﬃcients as possible with

1We call a regression coeﬃcient signiﬁcant if |bβj/ bsej| ≥2, where ˆβj is the estimated
(univariate) coeﬃcient and bsej is its estimated standard error.
2For a ﬁxed value of the regularization parameter λ, the degrees of freedom depends
on the observed predictor values in each simulation. Hence we compute the average
degrees of freedom over simulations.

concepts.name: thresholded partial least squares
concepts.description: A variant of partial least squares where features are thresholded based on their correlation with the outcome before applying PLS, similar to supervised principal components.
concepts.existing_quote: We select the features as in steps (1) and (2a) of supervised principal components, but then apply PLS (rather than principal components) to these features. For our current discussion, we call this "thresholded PLS."

concepts.name: pre-conditioning for interpretability
concepts.description: A method for finding a reduced set of features that approximates a model when the threshold yields too many features for interpretation.
concepts.existing_quote: Then for interpretational purposes, as well as for practical uses, we would like some way of finding a reduced a set of features that approximates the model. Pre-conditioning (Section 18.6.3) is one way of doing this.

concepts.name: pre-conditioned lasso
concepts.description: A variant of the lasso where the outcome variable is first denoised using supervised principal components, then the lasso is applied with the denoised outcome as the target. All features are used in the lasso fit, not just those retained in thresholding.
concepts.existing_quote: In this approach, one first computes the supervised principal component predictor ŷi for each observation in the training set (with the threshold selected by cross-validation). Then we apply the lasso with ŷi as the outcome variable, in place of the usual outcome yi.

concepts.name: permutation distribution for hypothesis testing
concepts.description: A nonparametric approach to computing p-values by permuting sample labels and computing test statistics for each permutation, avoiding distributional assumptions.
concepts.existing_quote: An attractive alternative approach is to use the permutation distribution, since it avoids assumptions about the distribution of the data. We compute (in principle) all K = (58 choose 14) permutations of the sample labels, and for each permutation k compute the t-statistics tk_j.

concepts.name: family-wise error rate
concepts.description: The probability of making at least one false rejection among all hypothesis tests, a conservative measure of overall error in multiple testing.
concepts.existing_quote: The family-wise error rate (FWER) is the probability of at least one false rejection, and is a commonly used overall measure of error.

concepts.name: benjamini-hochberg procedure
concepts.description: A procedure for controlling false discovery rate at level α by ordering p-values and finding the largest index where p(j) ≤ jα/M, then rejecting all hypotheses with smaller p-values.
concepts.existing_quote: Benjamini and Hochberg (1995) first proposed the notion of false discovery rate, and gave a testing procedure (Algorithm 18.2) whose FDR is bounded by a user-defined level α.

This is page 649

18

concepts.name: plug-in estimate of false discovery rate
concepts.description: A direct estimate of FDR computed as the average number of permutation values exceeding a threshold divided by the number of observed values exceeding that threshold.
concepts.existing_quote: The total number of permutation values |tk_j| equal or greater than 4.101 is 1518, for an average of 1518/1000 = 1.518 per permutation. Thus a direct estimate of the false discovery rate is FDR̂ = 1.518/11 ≈ 14%.
concepts.existing_formula: \widehat{\text{FDR}} = \frac{\text{# permutation values} \geq C}{\text{# observed values} \geq C}

concepts.name: significance analysis of microarrays
concepts.description: A method for computing asymmetric significance cutpoints by plotting ordered test statistics against expected order statistics from permutations and finding where points leave a band of width Δ from the 45° line.
concepts.existing_quote: The significance analysis of microarrays (SAM) approach offers a way of doing this. The basis of the SAM method is shown in Figure 18.20. On the vertical axis we have plotted the ordered test statistics t(1) ≤ t(2) ≤ ... ≤ t(M), while the horizontal axis shows the expected order statistics from the permutations of the data.

and we can identify as many of the signiﬁcant coeﬃcients as possible with

1We call a regression coeﬃcient signiﬁcant if |bβj/ bsej| ≥2, where ˆβj is the estimated
(univariate) coeﬃcient and bsej is its estimated standard error.
2For a ﬁxed value of the regularization parameter λ, the degrees of freedom depends
on the observed predictor values in each simulation. Hence we compute the average
degrees of freedom over simulations.

concepts.name: positive false discovery rate
concepts.description: A variant of FDR defined as E[V/R | R > 0], the expected false discovery rate conditional on making at least one rejection.
concepts.existing_quote: First we need to define the positive false discovery rate (pFDR) as

paragraphs.text:
This is page 649
Printer: Opaque this
18
High-Dimensional Problems: p ≫N

In this chapter we discuss prediction problems in which the number of
features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written
p ≫N. Such problems have become of increasing importance, especially in
genomics and other areas of computational biology. We will see that high
variance and overﬁtting are a major concern in this setting. As a result,
simple, highly regularized approaches often become the methods of choice.
The ﬁrst part of the chapter focuses on prediction in both the classiﬁcation
and regression settings, while the second part discusses the more basic
problem of feature selection and assessment.
To get us started, Figure 18.1 summarizes a small simulation study that
demonstrates the “less ﬁtting is better” principle that applies when p ≫N.
For each of N = 100 samples, we generated p standard Gaussian features
X with pairwise correlation 0.2. The outcome Y was generated according
to a linear model

where ε was generated from a standard Gaussian distribution. For each
dataset, the set of coeﬃcients βj were also generated from a standard Gaus-
sian distribution. We investigated three cases: p = 20, 100, and 1000. The
standard deviation σ was chosen in each case so that the signal-to-noise
ratio Var[E(Y |X)])/Var(ε) equaled 2. As a result, the number of signiﬁcant

univariate regression coeﬃcients1 was 9, 33 and 331, respectively, averaged
over the 100 simulation runs. The p = 1000 case is designed to mimic the
kind of data that we might see in a high-dimensional genomic or proteomic
dataset, for example.
We ﬁt a ridge regression to the data, with three diﬀerent values for the
regularization parameter λ: 0.001, 100, and 1000. When λ = 0.001, this
is nearly the same as least squares regression, with a little regularization
just to ensure that the problem is non-singular when p > N. Figure 18.1
shows boxplots of the relative test error achieved by the diﬀerent estimators
in each scenario. The corresponding average degrees of freedom used in
each ridge-regression ﬁt is indicated (computed using formula (3.50) on
page 682). The degrees of freedom is a more interpretable parameter than
λ. We see that ridge regression with λ = 0.001 (20 df) wins when p = 20;
λ = 100 (35 df) wins when p = 100, and λ = 1000 (43 df) wins when
p = 1000,
Here is an explanation for these results. When p = 20, we ﬁt all the way
and we can identify as many of the signiﬁcant coeﬃcients as possible with

1We call a regression coeﬃcient signiﬁcant if |bβj/ bsej| ≥2, where ˆβj is the estimated
(univariate) coeﬃcient and bsej is its estimated standard error.
2For a ﬁxed value of the regularization parameter λ, the degrees of freedom depends
on the observed predictor values in each simulation. Hence we compute the average
degrees of freedom over simulations.

concepts.name: mixture model for hypothesis testing
concepts.description: A Bayesian framework where each test statistic comes from either the null distribution F0 with probability π0 or the alternative distribution F1 with probability 1-π0.
concepts.existing_quote: We define a random variable Zj which equals 0 if the jth null hypothesis is true, and 1 otherwise. We assume that each pair (tj, Zj) are i.i.d random variables with
